---
title: "Regular Expressions for Text Processing"
subtitle: "Find patterns in text with precision for DH workflows."
author: "Curt Rode"
date: last-modified
difficulty: intermediate
time-estimate: "45 min"
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

Regular expressions (regex) are one of the most powerful tools for working with text. If you’ve ever needed to find all the dates in a document, extract email addresses, or clean messy OCR output, regex makes it possible at scale.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Learning Objectives
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

- Read basic regex patterns
- Write patterns for common DH tasks
- Use character classes and quantifiers
- Apply regex in Python
- Test and debug your patterns
:::

## Why Regex Matters

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="regex-why">
  <h3>Quick Check: When to Use Regex</h3>
  <p>You have 500 files with dates in different formats. What's the best approach?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="regex-quiz-1" value="manual">
    Find each date manually
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="regex-quiz-1" value="regex">
    Write regex patterns to match each format and extract them
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="regex-quiz-1" value="ai">
    Ask AI to scan all files and summarize
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Exactly!</strong> Regex is fast, precise, and repeatable at scale.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p>Regex gives you precise, reusable patterns for large-scale text tasks.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## The Basics

### Literal Matching
The pattern `cat` matches the exact characters “cat” wherever they appear — even inside other words like “catalog.”

### Special Characters (Metacharacters)

| Pattern | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `.` | Any single character | `c.t` matches `cat`, `cot` |
| `^` | Start of line | `^The` |
| `$` | End of line | `end$` |
| `\\d` | Any digit | `\\d\\d\\d` |
| `\\w` | Word character | `\\w+` |
| `\\s` | Whitespace | `\\s+` |

### Quantifiers

| Quantifier | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `*` | Zero or more | `ab*c` |
| `+` | One or more | `ab+c` |
| `?` | Optional | `colou?r` |
| `{n}` | Exactly n | `\\d{4}` |
| `{n,m}` | Between n and m | `\\d{2,4}` |

### Character Classes

| Class | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `[aeiou]` | Any vowel | |
| `[A-Z]` | Any capital letter | |
| `[^0-9]` | Not a digit | |

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="character-classes">
  <h3>Quick Check: Character Classes</h3>
  <p>Which pattern matches a capitalized word?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="cc-quiz" value="a">
    <code>[A-Z]</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="cc-quiz" value="b">
    <code>[A-Z][a-z]+</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="cc-quiz" value="c">
    <code>[A-Z]+</code>
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Correct!</strong> One capital followed by lowercase letters matches a typical capitalized word.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p><code>[A-Z]</code> is just one letter; <code>[A-Z]+</code> matches ALL CAPS.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Practical DH Examples

**Dates like “January 15, 1842”:**
```
(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)\s+\d{1,2},?\s+\d{4}
```

**Prices like “£450” or “$12.99”:**
```
[$£€]\d+(?:\.\d{2})?
```

**Names with titles:**
```
(Mr|Mrs|Miss|Ms|Dr|Rev|Prof)\.?\s+[A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)?
```

## Regex in Python

```python
import re

text = "Contact us at info@example.com or support@example.org"
emails = re.findall(r'\S+@\S+', text)
print(emails)
```

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="python-regex">
  <h3>Quick Check: Python Regex</h3>
  <p>You want all 4-digit years in a document. Which is correct?</p>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="py-quiz" value="a">
    <code>re.search(r'\\d{4}', text)</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="py-quiz" value="b">
    <code>re.findall(r'\\b\\d{4}\\b', text)</code>
  </label>
  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="py-quiz" value="c">
    <code>re.sub(r'\\d{4}', '', text)</code>
  </label>
  <div class="feedback-correct">
    <p><strong>Correct!</strong> <code>findall</code> returns every match, and word boundaries avoid partial matches.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="feedback-incorrect">
    <p><code>search</code> finds only the first match; <code>sub</code> replaces text.</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Testing and Debugging

Recommended tools:
- <https://regex101.com>
- <https://regexr.com>
- <https://pythex.org>

Strategy:
1. Start simple  
2. Add complexity gradually  
3. Test edge cases  

## Practice Exercise

Sample text:
```
London, January 15, 1842

Dear Mr. Smith,

The asking price of £450 seems reasonable,
though Dr. Watson suggests we offer £400.
```

Try to extract:
1. Dates  
2. People with titles  
3. Prices  

<details>
<summary>Show solutions</summary>

```
(January|February|March|April|May|June|July|August|September|October|November|December)\s+\d{1,2},?\s+\d{4}
(Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+[A-Z][a-z]+
£\d+
```
</details>

## Next Steps

Regex is a skill you refine with practice. Keep a personal “pattern library” and test with real data.
